title: From Detection to Safety: Reframing Fault Simulation for Functional Safety - Semiwiki
gist: Fault simulation is being reframed from a manufacturing-defect detection tool into a functional-safety verification methodology, where the constraint is validating FMEDA safety-mechanism coverage under ISO 26262 across system-level, software-driven fault campaigns. Because fault spaces reach into the billions and pure simulation can take months, Synopsys pairs simulation (VC Z01X) with hardware emulation (ZeBu) under a unified fault database to compress coverage closure from months to hours.

insights.claim: Classical fault simulation was optimized entirely around a manufacturing worldview in which faults are treated as random, independent, undesirable physical defects while the design itself is assumed functionally correct.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Legacy EDA test tooling was never architected for operational safety, opening a greenfield for vendors who can re-tool fault simulation for functional-safety use cases.
insights.quote: Faults were random, independent, and undesirable deviations from an otherwise correct design.
insights.locator: But this paradigm was fundamentally rooted

insights.claim: The core abstraction that made classical fault simulation scalable was modeling analog physical defects as logical stuck-at-0 and stuck-at-1 conditions at the gate or board level, letting engineers reason about defect coverage without transistor-level detail.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Understanding this abstraction explains why legacy tools break down at system level, where timing and software context matter and simple stuck-at models are insufficient.
insights.quote: Physical defects, with all their analog complexity, were modeled as logical faults, most notably stuck-at-0 and stuck-at-1 conditions at the gate or board level.
insights.locator: At the heart of this approach was abstraction

insights.claim: Classical simulation engines like Teradyne's LASAR were engineered for throughput rather than introspection, using parallel and concurrent fault simulation to evaluate thousands of faults against common test vectors, with the goal of detecting and identifying faults rather than understanding system behavior under fault.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: The throughput-vs-introspection tradeoff defines why a new class of tools is needed for safety, where behavioral understanding under fault is the whole point.
insights.quote: Simulation engines were engineered for throughput, not introspection.
insights.locator: Equally important was performance

insights.claim: In functional safety the governing question shifts from whether a defect can be detected before shipping to whether the system can tolerate, mitigate, or safely respond to faults during operation, treating faults as an inherent part of the operational environment rather than manufacturing artifacts.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: This reframing expands the addressable EDA market from manufacturing test into the full product lifecycle of safety-critical semiconductors.
insights.quote: In functional safety, the question is no longer whether a defect can be detected, but whether the system can tolerate, mitigate, or safely respond to faults during operation.
insights.locator: From Manufacturing Defects to Functional Safety

insights.claim: The shift to functional safety changes the metrics from fault coverage to safety coverage, the artifacts from fault dictionaries to safety cases, and the simulation context from isolated logic evaluation to full-system behavioral validation under fault conditions.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Each of these transitions is a distinct product surface where incumbent test-focused tools must be replaced or extended.
insights.quote: The metrics change, from fault coverage to safety coverage. The artifacts evolve, from fault dictionaries to safety cases.
insights.locator: This shift demands a rethinking

insights.claim: Under ISO 26262, adequate safety verification requires knowing not just that a fault is detectable but when it is detected, how it propagates, how long it remains latent, and whether the system transitions into a safe state within required time bounds.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: These temporal requirements make static gate-level tools inadequate and create demand for time-aware, running-system fault campaigns.
insights.quote: one must also understand when it is detected, under what conditions it becomes active, how long it remains latent, and whether the system transitions into a safe state within the required time bounds.
insights.locator: In this context, the notion of fault

insights.claim: Datacenter and networking applications address faults through the lens of Reliability, Availability, and Serviceability (RAS), extended across the product lifecycle via Silicon Lifecycle Management (SLM) providing continuous monitoring, optimization, diagnosis, and debug from deployment through field operation.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: SLM extends fault-related EDA revenue past tapeout into recurring in-field monitoring, a structurally more attractive business than one-time design tools.
insights.quote: This lifecycle perspective is further enabled by Silicon Lifecycle Management (SLM), which provides continuous monitoring, optimization, diagnosis, and debug capabilities from deployment through field operation.
insights.locator: Likewise, in datacenter and networking applications

insights.claim: FMEDA (Failure Modes, Effects, and Diagnostic Analysis) operates only at an abstract level as a model of expected behavior, decomposing a system, assigning failure modes, estimating occurrence rates, and attributing diagnostic coverage based on design intent rather than demonstrated behavior.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: The gap between FMEDA's paper claims and actual behavior is exactly the value pocket that fault simulation/emulation tools monetize.
insights.quote: It is a model of expected behavior, not a demonstration of actual behavior.
insights.locator: FMEDA provides the analytical backbone

insights.claim: The role of fault simulation and emulation in functional safety is to validate the assumptions embedded in FMEDA by injecting faults corresponding to modeled failure modes and measuring whether safety mechanisms deliver their credited coverage in practice.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Positions fault campaigns as the empirical evidence layer required to sign off a safety case, making them non-optional spend for safety-critical chip programs.
insights.quote: A safety mechanism may be credited with a certain level of coverage on paper, but that claim must be substantiated through experimentation.
insights.locator: This gap between assumption and evidence

insights.claim: Modern functional safety verification is built around fault injection campaigns that unfold over time within a running system while real software executes and realistic workloads are applied, where a fault may stay dormant, activate only under specific runtime conditions, and propagate across multiple subsystems before detection.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: The requirement to run real software during campaigns is what forces the move to hardware emulation and away from pure simulation.
insights.quote: Faults are introduced while real software is executing, realistic workloads are applied, and the system interacts with its environment.
insights.locator: Modern functional safety verification, by contrast

insights.claim: Modern vehicles use highly distributed architectures integrating multiple SoCs and ECUs from different vendors for perception, control, actuation, and user interaction, so safety depends on coordinated cross-ECU behavior rather than any single component.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Multi-vendor distributed architectures make system-level safety verification a shared cross-supplier problem, favoring tool vendors who own the unified end-to-end flow.
insights.quote: Today's vehicles are composed of highly distributed architectures, integrating multiple SoCs and ECUs from different vendors.
insights.locator: Nowhere is this complexity more evident

insights.claim: Fault lists for modern designs routinely reach into the millions, and even after fault reduction and optimization the space is vast, with the combinatorial explosion across multiple ECUs pushing the effective fault space into the billions.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: A billions-scale fault space is the quantitative driver that makes acceleration hardware (emulation) a necessity, not a luxury, sizing the compute demand.
insights.quote: When extended across multiple ECUs, the combinatorial explosion can push the effective fault space into the billions.
insights.locator: Validating such end-to-end behaviors

insights.claim: Running system-level fault campaigns with full software stacks purely in simulation can take weeks or even months, an impractical proposition for safety-critical development programs where engineers need coverage results within days to iterate.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Time-to-results is the binding constraint that pulls budget from software simulation into emulation hardware, a clear demand signal for accelerators.
insights.quote: Running such campaigns purely in simulation can take weeks or even months, an impractical proposition for safety-critical development programs.
insights.locator: In principle, simulation can manage this challenge

insights.claim: The industry has converged on a hybrid methodology where faults local to an IP or single ECU are handled in simulation for modeling flexibility and observability, while faults propagating across subsystems, ECUs, and software layers are offloaded to emulation for execution speed.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: The winning vendor is the one selling both engines plus the glue, capturing the full flow rather than a point tool.
insights.quote: The industry has therefore converged into a hybrid methodology.
insights.locator: This is precisely where fault emulation

insights.claim: The hybrid approach is unified under a single fault campaign backed by a shared fault database that serves as common input and output across simulation, formal analysis, and emulation engines, enabling seamless partitioning without disrupting the user flow.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: The unified fault database is the lock-in moat: whoever owns it controls the flow and makes competitor point tools hard to substitute.
insights.quote: A shared fault database serves as both input and output across simulation, formal analysis, and emulation engines.
insights.locator: Crucially, this hybrid approach is unified

insights.claim: Because fault simulation has been the foundation of safety verification for decades, emulation must adhere to the same use model with similar fault definitions, campaign structures, and reporting formats so users can move between engines without reauthoring flows.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Compatibility with decades-old simulation flows is a switching-cost advantage for incumbents like Synopsys over new entrants.
insights.quote: emulation must adhere to the same use model, supporting similar fault definitions, campaign structures, and reporting formats.
insights.locator: At the same time, consistency between simulation and emulation

insights.claim: In emulation, certain fault models involving timing semantics or detailed memory behavior must be adapted because memories may reside off-chip and be accessed through physical interfaces, shifting where faults can be meaningfully injected or observed.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: These implementation constraints define the technical moat and the residual role for simulation, so neither engine fully displaces the other.
insights.quote: memories may reside off-chip and be accessed through physical interfaces, shifting the point at which faults can be meaningfully injected or observed.
insights.locator: There are, of course, differences in implementation

insights.claim: On a RISC-V reference design, Synopsys VC Z01X simulation estimated a Linux boot runtime of over 4 months versus 7 hours 22 minutes on ZeBu emulation, demonstrating an orders-of-magnitude speedup for software-driven fault campaigns.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: A months-to-hours delta quantifies why safety programs will pay for emulation hardware, directly supporting Synopsys hardware-emulation revenue.
insights.quote:
Runtime (Linux Boot)
> 4 months (estimated)
7hr 22 min
insights.locator: Table I: Benchmark results

insights.claim: On a SoC design, VC Z01X ran a Linux boot fault campaign in 39hr 25min while ZeBu completed it in 4hr 52min on a single host and just 58 minutes across 8 hosts, showing near-linear multi-host scaling of emulation.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Multi-host scaling (39hr to 58min) implies customers buy multiple emulation hosts to hit safety deadlines, expanding hardware attach per design.
insights.quote:
39hr 25min
4hr 52min@1Host
58min@8Hosts
insights.locator: Table I: Benchmark results

insights.claim: In the SoC benchmark, 133 hard faults classified as Hyper Faults were dropped from fault simulation, of which emulation ultimately detected 101 and left 32 not-detected, illustrating coverage closure unreachable by simulation alone.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Emulation surfacing 101 of 133 dropped faults shows tangible coverage gains that translate into stronger, more defensible safety cases.
insights.quote:
133 (Hyper Faults) Dropped from Fault Simulation
Detected: 101
Not-detected: 32
insights.locator: Fault Results

insights.claim: The article argues fault emulation has been elevated from a mere performance optimization to a critical enabler of functional safety verification, but only when integrated into a broader software-defined safety flow with automated fault classification, safety metric calculation, campaign management, and traceability.
insights.speaker: Lauro Rizzatti
insights.so_what: Value accrues to the integrated software-defined flow, not raw hardware speed, favoring full-stack EDA suites over standalone emulators.
insights.quote: The value lies not merely in the speed of the underlying hardware engines, but in their integration into a broader software-defined safety verification flow.
insights.locator: Yet fault emulation alone is not enough
